Option A treats dividing by 1/4 like dividing by 4. Option C uses the wrong idea about dividing by a fraction: dividing by a number less than 1 makes the answer larger. Option D reaches 20 for an invalid reason because adding is not the operation asked for.

questionText: Look at these two methods for a water tank problem. Method P says 1/6 litre shared equally into 3 cups gives 1/18 litre per cup. Method Q says 1/6 litre shared equally into 3 cups gives 3/6 litre per cup because there are 3 cups. Decide which judgment is correct.
subject: Maths
learningObjective: Students can divide a unit fraction by a whole number and a whole number by a unit fraction
summary: MCQ for Grade 5 Fraction Arithmetic: Dividing Fractions, aligned to Students can divide a unit fraction by a whole number and a whole number by a unit fraction.
options: [{"text":"Only Method P is correct because sharing a small fraction into more equal parts makes each part smaller.","correct":true},{"text":"Only Method Q is correct because 3 cups means multiply the numerator by 3.","correct":false},{"text":"Both methods are correct because 1/18 and 3/6 are equivalent fractions.","correct":false},{"text":"Neither method is correct because 1/6 cannot be divided by 3.","correct":false}]
explanation: Method P is correct: 1/6 ÷ 3 = 1/18. Sharing one sixth among 3 cups means each cup gets a smaller amount. Method Q is wrong because it makes the amount bigger instead of smaller. Also, 1/18 and 3/6 are not equivalent. Division of fractions like this is allowed, so Option D is false.

questionText: Choose the method that correctly solves this ticket problem: a hall has 8 whole prize packs, and each winner receives 1/2 of a pack. The organiser wants the number of winners and checks four methods below.
subject: Maths
learningObjective: Students can divide a unit fraction by a whole number and a whole number by a unit fraction
summary: MCQ for Grade 5 Fraction Arithmetic: Dividing Fractions, aligned to Students can divide a unit fraction by a whole number and a whole number by a unit fraction.
options: [{"text":"Use 8 ÷ 1/2 = 16, because the question asks how many half-packs fit into 8 packs.","correct":true},{"text":"Use 1/2 ÷ 8 = 1/16, because each winner gets half and there are 8 packs.","correct":false},{"text":"Use 8 ÷ 2 = 4, because dividing by one-half is the same as dividing by 2.","correct":false},{"text":"Use 8 + 1/2 = 8 1/2, because each winner gets an extra half-pack.","correct":false}]
explanation: The organiser needs the number of half-pack groups in 8 packs, so the correct method is 8 ÷ 1/2 = 16. Option B reverses the division and answers a different question. Option C confuses dividing by one-half with dividing by 2; dividing by one-half doubles the number of groups. Option D uses addition, which does not match the grouping situation.
